media.alt: City gate of Tabriz, drawn by Eugène Flandin during his 1839–1841 visit — the gate Edward Burgess would have entered
media.caption: City Gate, Tabriz — Eugène Flandin, c. 1840

media.alt: House of Hussein Khan in Tabriz — a wealthy residence of the kind Edward moved among
media.caption: A Tabriz merchant's house — Flandin, c. 1840

media.alt: Portrait of Charles Burgess, Edward's elder brother, whose debts trapped Edward in Persia
media.caption: Charles Burgess — the brother who left him behind

media.alt: Remains of the Palace Zobeideh in Tabriz — ruins near the villages Edward governed
media.caption: Ruins at Tabriz — Flandin, c. 1840

media.alt: Naser al-Din Shah Qajar, the young ruler who appointed Edward chief interpreter, state gazette editor, press censor, and intelligence briefer
media.caption: Naser al-Din Shah — photograph by Nadar

media.alt: Anna Saginian with her daughter Fanny Burgess, photographed in 1881
media.caption: Anna and Fanny — photographed twenty-six years after Edward's death

media.alt: A takhtrawan — the mule-borne litter in which Anna carried the dying Edward from Tehran to Tabriz
media.caption: The takhtrawan — 'often movement gave him such agony that he looked as dead'

media.alt: The Armenian cemetery just outside the walls of Tabriz — where Edward Burgess was buried in 1855
media.caption: The Armenian cemetery, Tabriz — Edward's resting place since 1855
